title: A resolution recognizing the 80th anniversary of the amphibious landing on the Japanese island of Iwo Jima during World War II and the raisings of the flag of the United States on Mount Suribachi.
plain_summary: This resolution honors the eightieth anniversary of an important World War Two battle that happened on a Japanese island called Iwo Jima in 1945. The resolution celebrates the brave American Marines, Sailors, Soldiers, and Coast Guard members who fought in this battle, including the famous moment when they raised the American flag on Mount Suribachi. It also recognizes that the Marine Corps and Navy are celebrating their 250th anniversaries this year. The Senate wants to celebrate the friendship between the United States and Japan today, and it encourages Americans to honor these veterans with special ceremonies and activities.
last_action_text: Resolution agreed to in Senate without amendment and with a preamble by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S1037; text: 2/4/2025 CR S597-598)

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution recognizes the eightieth anniversary of the amphibious landing on the Japanese island of Iwo Jima during World War Two and the raisings of the flag of the United States on Mount Suribachi.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: After Japanese forces attacked Pearl Harbor, Hawaii on December seventh, nineteen forty one, the United States declared war on Japan on December eighth, nineteen forty one. For the next four years, the United States and Allied forces fought back against Japanese advances across the Pacific region. The military used a tactic called island hopping, which meant attacking and taking control of Japanese-held islands one by one using amphibious assault landings. The goal was to secure airfields and supply bases to extend United States Navy control westward across the Pacific Ocean toward Japan, launch bombing attacks against mainland Japan using the new Boeing B-29 Superfortress aircraft, and prepare for a possible United States invasion of Japan. By early nineteen forty five, United States and Allied forces had advanced to the island of Iwo Jima, an eight square mile island of volcanic rock and sand with three strategic airfields, located between the Mariana Islands and Japan. Iwo Jima was a strategic island with airfields to support United States bombers with fighter escorts and was an essential base for emergency, refueling, and backup landings for B-29 bombers. Under the command of Japanese Lieutenant General Tadamichi Kuribayashi, Iwo Jima was heavily fortified with nearly eleven miles of underground tunnels, rooms, bunkers, artillery positions, ammunition storage areas, and pillboxes supporting more than twenty one thousand Japanese soldiers. On February nineteenth, nineteen forty five, under the leadership of several United States military commanders, the United States launched an amphibious landing and assault on Iwo Jima called the Battle of Iwo Jima. This battle involved more than seventy thousand members of the United States Marine Corps, supported by thousands of members of the United States Navy, Coast Guard, and Army serving as assault, garrison, and support forces. The Marines who fought in the Battle of Iwo Jima overcame many disadvantages during the thirty six day battle including dangerous terrain, bad weather conditions, and heavy enemy fire from an entrenched and determined Japanese fighting force in places the Marines named the Meat Grinder and Bloody Gorge. On February twenty third, nineteen forty five, only five days into the Battle of Iwo Jima, members of the United States Marine Corps climbed the highest point on the island, Mount Suribachi, and raised the United States flag two times. The second flag raising resulted in the famous Pulitzer Prize winning photograph taken by photographer Joe Rosenthal. This image has become a recognized symbol of determination, perseverance, and struggle. It has been memorialized as the United States Marine Corps War Memorial in Arlington, Virginia. The photograph immortalized the fighting spirit of the United States Marine Corps and the heroism of the six Marine flag raisers shown. It has continued to inspire the people of the United States to fight in support of our nation's interests and allies, even against difficult odds. Secretary of the Navy James Forrestal, who was watching the battle from the sea aboard a landing craft, remarked to Lieutenant General Holland M. Smith that the raising of that flag on Suribachi means a Marine Corps for the next five hundred years. The Battle of Iwo Jima was one of the bloodiest battles in the history of the United States Marine Corps, resulting in more than twenty six thousand casualties for the United States, more than six thousand eight hundred of whom were killed. The Battle of Iwo Jima led to twenty two members of the United States Marine Corps and five members of the United States Navy receiving the Medal of Honor. This represents the most members of the United States Marine Corps ever to receive the highest military decoration in the United States for a single battle and more than one fourth of all members of the United States Marine Corps to receive the decoration during World War Two. The secured airfields on Iwo Jima became emergency landing locations for two thousand two hundred fifty one B-29 Bombers, saving the lives of an estimated twenty four thousand seven hundred sixty one crewmen. One hundred sixty days after the end and victory of the important Battle of Iwo Jima, the United States received the unconditional surrender of Japan on September second, nineteen forty five. The world owes a debt of gratitude to the members of the United States Marine Corps who selflessly led the fight for the strategic island of Iwo Jima in the middle of the Pacific theater. The eightieth anniversary of the Battle of Iwo Jima will be marked by commemorative events on the island of Iwo Jima, in Washington DC, and internationally, organized by the people of the United States and Japan.

sections.title: What the Senate Resolves
sections.text: The Senate recognizes the eightieth anniversary of the amphibious landing on the Japanese island of Iwo Jima that began on February nineteenth, nineteen forty five, and ended on March twenty sixth, nineteen forty five. The Senate commemorates the famous and historic raisings of the flag of the United States on Mount Suribachi that occurred on February twenty third, nineteen forty five. The Senate honors the Marines, Sailors, Soldiers, Army Air Crew, and Coast Guardsmen who fought bravely on Iwo Jima, including the thousands of Japanese soldiers who stubbornly defended the island. The Senate remembers and honors the service members who gave their lives on the battlefield. The Senate recognizes the Allied victory at the Battle of Iwo Jima, which was led by the United States Marine Corps and made the defeat of the Empire of Japan in World War Two possible. The Senate affirms the memorable words of Admiral Chester Nimitz, who stated that uncommon valor was a common virtue among the service members of the United States who fought on Iwo Jima. The Senate reaffirms the bonds of friendship and shared values between the United States and Japan, whose strong alliance demonstrates the power of reconciliation between former enemies. The Senate encourages the people of the United States to honor the veterans of the Battle of Iwo Jima with appropriate programs, ceremonies, and activities. The Senate honors the service and sacrifice of the men and women who serve the United States today, carrying on the proud tradition of those who came before them. The Senate salutes the two hundred fiftieth year since the founding of the United States Marine Corps and the United States Navy.
